title: Beeswax pillar candle with frankincense
subtitle: A slow-burning pillar scented with frankincense essential oil
excerpt: A hand-poured beeswax pillar candle scented with frankincense essential oil. The resinous, warm scent of frankincense complements the natural honey note of beeswax. Beeswax burns slower and at a higher temperature than paraffin; the pillar holds its shape well and drips very little.
sourceNotes: Beeswax melt point 62 to 65°C. Frankincense essential oil flash point above 90°C; suitable for beeswax pillar pour temperatures of 70 to 75°C. Pillar beeswax fragrance load typically 3 to 5% (lower than container candles because beeswax already has a strong natural scent and higher loading can cause seeping). British Beekeepers Association practical guides; Bramble Berry candle technical notes.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 pillar candle (approximately 250 g, ~10 cm tall × 6 cm diameter)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale wax weight for your mould volume. Essential oil percentage stays at 3 to 4% of wax weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow 12 hours in the mould before unmoulding. Pillar candles benefit from 1 to 2 days of rest after unmoulding before their first burn.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Beeswax pillar candles keep indefinitely. A white surface bloom develops over time; this is harmless and can be polished off. The frankincense scent persists for years in an unlit candle stored away from light and heat.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a wax fully liquefies. Beeswax has a higher melt point (62 to 65°C) than soy or paraffin, making beeswax pillars durable and slow-burning.
A white powdery film that develops on beeswax candle surfaces over weeks or months. Caused by wax component migration; harmless and disappears when the candle burns.
The temperature at which a fragrance or essential oil can ignite near a flame. Frankincense has a flash point above 90°C, well above any beeswax pour temperature.

content.text: Beeswax pillar candles need more care than jar candles. The wax must be poured at the right temperature, the wick must be sized for the diameter, and a second pour is needed because beeswax shrinks on cooling. The result is a candle with a high
that holds its shape well, burns slowly, and carries the natural honey note of the wax alongside the frankincense. Frankincense is a natural partner for beeswax. Its
is above 90°C, well above beeswax pour temperatures. The fragrance load is 3.5% of wax weight; higher loading in beeswax can cause the oil to seep out of the finished pillar.

items.prepNote: Yellow or white pellets; yellow has a stronger natural honey note
3.5% of wax weight; resinous, warm note
Sized for a 6 cm diameter pillar; ECO-8 or CD-14 as a starting point; adjust for your mould diameter

content.text: Thread the wick through the base hole of the pillar mould, feeding it from inside out. Secure the wick at the base with a wick pin or small piece of tape so it cannot pull through during pouring. Pull the wick taut at the top and secure it across the centring bar resting on the mould opening. Warm the mould slightly if it is metal or cold glass to reduce pillar lines.

Weigh the beeswax into the pouring pitcher and melt over a water bath or low direct heat. Do not leave unattended. Heat to 73 to 75°C on the probe thermometer. Remove from heat, add the frankincense essential oil, and stir to combine. The oil does not need to cool before adding at these temperatures.

Pour the scented beeswax into the mould in a steady stream, keeping the wick centred. Fill to 1 cm below the top. Leave undisturbed; beeswax shrinks significantly on cooling and the centre will sink. After 2 to 3 hours, top up with a second pour of the same wax heated to 73°C. Repeat if a third top-up is needed. Leave the mould undisturbed for 12 hours total.

After 12 hours, the beeswax will have contracted away from the mould sides. Flex the mould gently if silicone, or cool briefly in the fridge (15 minutes) if metal. Pull the pillar free. Trim the wick at the base flush with the pillar bottom and to 5 mm at the top. A light
may develop on the surface within days; this is normal. Allow the pillar to rest for 1 to 2 days before its first burn.

Always burn pillar candles on a heat-resistant plate or pillar holder that can contain wax if any drips. Keep away from draughts; beeswax is less draft-tolerant than paraffin and a draft can cause uneven burning. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each lighting. Beeswax pillars should be burned in sessions of 1 hour per 2.5 cm of pillar diameter to form an even pool without tunnelling.
Beeswax pillars keep indefinitely. Surface bloom can be polished off with a soft cloth or a nylon stocking. The frankincense scent persists for years in unlit, dark-stored candles.
